еңЁ(г„—г„һЛӢ) и…Ұ(г„Ӣг„ ЛҮ) зӯӢ(г„җг„§г„Ј) жҖҘ(г„җг„§ЛҠ) иҪү(г„“г„Ёг„ўЛҮ) еҪҺ(г„Ёг„ў) зҡ„(г„үг„ңЛҷ) еҘҮ(г„‘г„§ЛҠ) е№»(г„Ҹг„Ёг„ўЛӢ) дё–(г„•ЛӢ) з•Ң(г„җг„§г„қЛӢ) иЈЎ(г„Ңг„§ЛҮ) пјҢ иҗҠ(г„Ңг„һЛҠ) иҺү(г„Ңг„§ЛӢ) зҡ„(г„үг„ңЛҷ) жҖ§(г„’г„§г„ҘЛӢ) ж ј(г„Қг„ңЛҠ) жҳҜ(г„•ЛӢ) з”ұ(г„§г„ЎЛҠ) дёҖ(г„§ЛҠ) еә§(г„—г„Ёг„ӣЛӢ) еә§(г„—г„Ёг„ӣЛӢ) еҖӢ(г„Қг„ңЛӢ) жҖ§(г„’г„§г„ҘЛӢ) е°Ҹ(г„’г„§г„ ЛҮ) еі¶(г„үг„ ЛҮ) ж”Ҝ(г„“) ж’җ(г„”г„Ҙ) зҡ„(г„үг„ңЛҷ) пјҢ еғҸ(г„’г„§г„ӨЛӢ) жҳҜ(г„•ЛӢ) жҗһ(г„Қг„ ЛҮ) з¬‘(г„’г„§г„ ЛӢ) е°Ҹ(г„’г„§г„ ЛҮ) еі¶(г„үг„ ЛҮ) гҖҒ иӘ (г„”г„ҘЛҠ) еҜҰ(г„•ЛҠ) е°Ҹ(г„’г„§г„ ЛҮ) еі¶(г„үг„ ЛҮ) е’Ң(г„Ҹг„ўЛӢ) е®¶(г„җг„§г„ҡ) еәӯ(г„Ҡг„§г„ҘЛҠ) е°Ҹ(г„’г„§г„ ЛҮ) еі¶(г„үг„ ЛҮ) гҖӮ йҖҷ(г„“г„ңЛӢ) дәӣ(г„’г„§г„қ) е°Ҹ(г„’г„§г„ ЛҮ) еі¶(г„үг„ ЛҮ) зҡ„(г„үг„ңЛҷ) иғҪ(г„Ӣг„ҘЛҠ) йҮҸ(г„Ңг„§г„ӨЛӢ) дҫҶ(г„Ңг„һЛҠ) жәҗ(г„©г„ўЛҠ) пјҢ е°ұ(г„җг„§г„ЎЛӢ) жҳҜ(г„•ЛӢ) йӮЈ(г„Ӣг„ҡЛӢ) дәӣ(г„’г„§г„қ) иў«(г„…г„ҹЛӢ) жЁҷ(г„…г„§г„ ) иЁҳ(г„җг„§ЛӢ) зӮә(г„Ёг„ҹЛҠ) ж ё(г„Ҹг„ңЛҠ) еҝғ(г„’г„§г„Ј) иЁҳ(г„җг„§ЛӢ) жҶ¶(г„§ЛӢ) зҡ„(г„үг„ңЛҷ) жҷӮ(г„•ЛҠ) еҲ»(г„Һг„ңЛӢ) гҖӮ е°Қ(г„үг„Ёг„ҹЛӢ) еӯё(г„’г„©г„қЛҠ) з”ҹ(г„•г„Ҙ) дҫҶ(г„Ңг„һЛҠ) иӘӘ(г„•г„Ёг„ӣ) пјҢ зҗҶ(г„Ңг„§ЛҮ) и§Ј(г„җг„§г„қЛҮ) йҖҷ(г„“г„ңЛӢ) дәӣ(г„’г„§г„қ) е°Ҹ(г„’г„§г„ ЛҮ) еі¶(г„үг„ ЛҮ) еҰӮ(г„–г„ЁЛҠ) дҪ•(г„Ҹг„ңЛҠ) йҒӢ(г„©г„ЈЛӢ) дҪң(г„—г„Ёг„ӣЛӢ) пјҢ иғҪ(г„Ӣг„ҘЛҠ) е№«(г„…г„Ө) еҠ©(г„“г„ЁЛӢ) жҲ‘(г„Ёг„ӣЛҮ) еҖ‘(г„Үг„ЈЛҷ) еңЁ(г„—г„һЛӢ) иӘІ(г„Һг„ңЛӢ) жҘӯ(г„§г„қЛӢ) иҲҮ(г„©ЛҮ) з”ҹ(г„•г„Ҙ) жҙ»(г„Ҹг„Ёг„ӣЛҠ) еЈ“(г„§г„ҡ) еҠӣ(г„Ңг„§ЛӢ) дёӯ(г„“г„Ёг„Ҙ) дҝқ(г„…г„ ЛҮ) жҢҒ(г„”ЛҠ) з©©(г„Ёг„ЈЛҮ) е®ҡ(г„үг„§г„ҘЛӢ) гҖӮ

en:
Alice was beginning to get very tired of sitting by her sister on the bank, and of having nothing to do. Once or twice she had peeped into the book her sister was reading, but it had no pictures or conversations in it. What is the use of a book, thought Alice, without pictures or conversations.
Suddenly, a White Rabbit with pink eyes ran close by her. There was nothing so very remarkable in that, nor did Alice think it so very much out of the way to hear the Rabbit say to itself, Oh dear, Oh dear, I shall be late.
When the Rabbit actually took a watch out of its waistcoat pocket, and looked at it, and then hurried on, Alice started to her feet. She ran across the field after it, and fortunately was just in time to see it pop down a large rabbit hole under the hedge.
In another moment down went Alice after it, never once considering how in the world she was to get out again.

Where the mind is without fear and the head is held high. Where knowledge is free. Where the world has not been broken up into fragments by narrow domestic walls. Where words come out from the depth of truth. Where tireless striving stretches its arms towards perfection. Where the clear stream of reason has not lost its way into the dreary desert sand of dead habit. Where the mind is led forward by thee into ever widening thought and action. Into that heaven of freedom, my Father, let my country awake.
This poem is a beautiful prayer for a better world where everyone can live with dignity and courage. It encourages us to keep learning and to be honest in everything we do.
By holding our heads high, we show that we are proud and brave. Let us work together to build a place where everyone is free to think and grow.

Everyone has feelings, and it is okay to feel different ways at different times. Sometimes you might feel very happy and full of energy. Other times, you might feel sad, angry, or even a little bit scared. Managing your emotions means learning how to understand these feelings and stay calm.
When you feel angry, your heart might beat faster and your face might get hot. Instead of shouting or throwing things, try to take a deep breath and count to ten. This gives your brain time to think before you act.
Talking to a friend, a parent, or a teacher about how you feel can also help a lot. Remember that feelings are like waves in the ocean; they come and go.
By learning to watch your feelings without letting them take over, you can become the master of your own heart. Being calm helps you make better choices every day.

Politeness is a simple way of showing respect to the people around us. There are two magic words that can make your day much better, which are please and thank you.
When you ask for something, saying please shows that you are being kind and not just giving orders. It makes people feel happy to help you.
When someone gives you something or helps you with a task, saying thank you shows that you appreciate their effort.
These simple words act like a bridge between people. They create a friendly atmosphere in the classroom, at home, and on the playground.
Politeness also includes listening when others are speaking and waiting for your turn. When we are polite, we make the world a more pleasant and peaceful place to live in. It is a small habit that brings very big rewards in our friendships and daily lives.

It is very simple, said the fox to the little prince. It is only with the heart that one can see rightly. What is essential is invisible to the eye. This famous lesson teaches us that the most important things in life are not things we can touch or buy. They are things like love, friendship, and kindness.
We should take time to look at the stars and enjoy the beauty of nature every day. Growing up does not mean we have to stop being curious or stop asking questions. We should always try to understand the world around us with a kind heart. Taking care of a single flower or being a good friend to someone makes our lives meaningful and bright.
Even though the world is very big and sometimes confusing, we can find true joy in the smallest things if we look with our hearts instead of just our eyes. Curiosity and kindness are the best tools for any adventure.

A great lion was sleeping peacefully in the bright sunshine. A little mouse ran over his huge paw and woke him up by accident. The lion was very angry and put his paw over the mouse to eat him. Please do not eat me, cried the poor mouse. If you let me go, I might be able to help you someday. The lion laughed loudly because he thought a tiny mouse could never help a big lion. However, he was kind and let the mouse go free.
Later, some hunters caught the lion in a strong rope net. The lion roared for help but could not get away. The little mouse heard him and ran to the net. She chewed the ropes with her sharp teeth. Soon, the lion was free again. Even a small friend can be a great friend, said the mouse with a smile.

Sometimes we have to make difficult choices in our lives.
Imagine walking on a path in a deep forest and coming to a fork in the road. Both paths look beautiful, but you can only choose one to travel on. One path might be well-worn because many people have walked there before. The other path might be grassy because fewer people have used it. Which one would you decide to take.
The famous poet Robert Frost wrote a poem about this situation. He decided to take the road that less people had traveled. He believed that making this unique choice made all the difference in his life. It takes courage to follow your own path instead of just following the crowd. Trust yourself when making your next big decision, and embrace the adventure that follows.

Have you ever wondered how a caterpillar turns into a colorful butterfly. It is a magical journey that happens in four main stages.
First, a mother butterfly lays tiny eggs on a leaf. Next, a hungry caterpillar hatches from the egg. It eats many leaves and grows very quickly. When it is done growing, the caterpillar builds a hard shell around its body. This special shell is called a chrysalis. Inside the chrysalis, the caterpillar changes its whole body. This is a very quiet and secret time. Finally, the chrysalis opens and a beautiful butterfly emerges with large wet wings.
The butterfly waits for the sun to dry its wings and then flies into the sky to drink sweet nectar from flowers. Nature is full of wonderful surprises like this transformation.

Winnie the Pooh is a very famous and thoughtful bear who lives in the Hundred Acre Wood. He has many good friends, like Piglet, Tigger, and Christopher Robin. Pooh teaches us important lessons about kindness and bravery in our daily lives.
Christopher Robin once told Pooh that he must always remember three things. He is braver than he believes, stronger than he seems, and smarter than he thinks. This is a powerful message for everyone to hear. We should always believe in ourselves, even when things are difficult.
Pooh also teaches us that a small act of kindness, like sharing a pot of honey or giving a warm hug to a sad friend, can make a very big difference. True friendship is about supporting each other through happy times and scary times.

Honesty is one of the best habits a person can have. Being honest means always telling the truth and being trustworthy in everything you do.
When you are honest, people know they can rely on you. They will respect you for who you are. Telling a lie might seem easier at first, especially if you have made a mistake, but it usually causes more problems later.
One lie often leads to another lie, and soon you have a tangled mess to deal with. If you make a mistake, it is much better to be brave and tell the truth about it immediately.
People might still be upset about the mistake, but they will admire your courage for telling the truth. Trust is like a fragile glass bowl; if you break it with lies, it is very hard to fix perfectly.
